name: V-First (Pyraminx)
description: A Pyraminx method that prioritizes building a V-shape (one solved edge) as the first step after tips, then solves the remaining edges. V-First is essentially the foundation shared by L4E, Bell, and other modern Pyraminx methods. As a standalone approach, V-First emphasizes flexible V selection and using intuitive or semi-algorithmic techniques to finish the last four edges. It is the standard intermediate Pyraminx framework.

steps.name: Orient Tips
steps.description: Twist all four tips to match their adjacent center colors. This trivial step can be handled at any point.
tips: Handle tips at any convenient moment
Tip orientation never affects other pieces

steps.name: Build V
steps.description: Solve one edge to create a V-shape — a solved edge with its two adjacent matching centers. The V is typically placed at the back of the Pyraminx. Color neutrality is important: choose whichever edge is easiest to place. The V can often be planned entirely during inspection.
tips: Be fully color-neutral for V selection
Plan the V completely during inspection
Choose the V that requires the fewest moves
Place the V at the back so you can see the remaining edges

steps.name: Solve Last 4 Edges
steps.description: Solve the remaining four edges. This can be done intuitively (for beginners) or with algorithms (for speed). The intuitive approach involves placing edges one at a time, while the algorithmic approach solves all four in one look. There are approximately 16 distinct cases.
tips: Start with intuitive solutions, then learn algorithms
Recognize edge positions and orientations quickly
Practice the most common cases first
Mirror algorithms reduce total learning load

pros: Flexible V selection allows easy starts
Scalable from intuitive to algorithmic solving
Foundation for more advanced Pyraminx methods (L4E, Bell)
cons: Intuitive L4E solving is slower than algorithmic approaches
Requires color neutrality for best results
Generic approach — specialized methods like Bell are faster at the top level
L4E recognition takes practice to master
